pattern: Since a valid arrangement only ever alternates odd/even, the count of completions from any partial prefix depends purely on how many odd and even values are still unused -- so the k-th arrangement can be built one position at a time by comparing k against block sizes made of factorials, never by generating permutations.
hints: Whether a full arrangement of the remaining odd and even values can alternate at all -- and in how many ways -- depends only on how MANY of each remain, never on which specific ones they are.

At the very first position either parity may lead, so weigh every still-unused value (odds and evens merged in plain numeric order); at every later position, the parity is already forced to be the opposite of the one just placed.

For a candidate value, work out how many full arrangements are still possible after placing it (a product of two factorials, one per remaining parity group, zero if the counts can no longer alternate); if k is small enough to fall inside that block, commit to the value, otherwise subtract the block from k and move to the next candidate.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, both parities compete for the lead
statement example, odd count exceeds even count
statement example, k beyond the total valid count
single value, only one arrangement exists
single value, k past the only arrangement
odd n forces the very first arrangement to start on an odd value
k equal to the exact total, must return the lexicographically largest arrangement, not empty
k one past the exact total, must return empty rather than an almost-valid arrangement
odd n with a mid-range k, exercises many forced alternation steps in a row

stress.description: random n across the full range with k chosen near, at, or occasionally just past the true total count
stress.note: total is computed exactly the same way the reference does, so k lands in-range almost always and occasionally just over to exercise the empty-result path

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def permute(self, n, k):
        fact = [1] * (n + 1)
        for i in range(1, n + 1):
            fact[i] = fact[i - 1] * i

        def count(oa, ea, parity):
            if oa < 0 or ea < 0:
                return 0
            if parity == 1:
                ok = (oa == ea) or (oa == ea + 1)
            else:
                ok = (ea == oa) or (ea == oa + 1)
            return fact[oa] * fact[ea] if ok else 0

        odds = list(range(1, n + 1, 2))
        evens = list(range(2, n + 1, 2))

        total = count(len(odds), len(evens), 1) + count(len(odds), len(evens), 0)
        if k > total:
            return []

        result = []
        required = None
        for _ in range(n):
            if required is None:
                candidates = sorted(odds + evens)
                found = False
                for val in candidates:
                    is_odd = val % 2 == 1
                    new_oa = len(odds) - (1 if is_odd else 0)
                    new_ea = len(evens) - (1 if not is_odd else 0)
                    next_parity = 0 if is_odd else 1
                    block = count(new_oa, new_ea, next_parity)
                    if block > 0 and k <= block:
                        result.append(val)
                        (odds if is_odd else evens).remove(val)
                        required = next_parity
                        found = True
                        break
                    k -= block
                if not found:
                    return []
            else:
                pool = odds if required == 1 else evens
                avail = len(pool)
                if avail == 0:
                    return []
                new_oa = len(odds) - (1 if required == 1 else 0)
                new_ea = len(evens) - (1 if required == 0 else 0)
                next_parity = 0 if required == 1 else 1
                block = count(new_oa, new_ea, next_parity)
                group_total = avail * block
                if block == 0 or k > group_total:
                    return []
                idx = (k - 1) // block
                k = (k - 1) % block + 1
                val = pool.pop(idx)
                result.append(val)
                required = next_parity
        return result
